text:
Powered byOutside

Belgium's Remco Evenepoel cycles to cross the finish line of the men's road cycling individual time trial during the Paris 2024 Olympic Games in Paris, on July 27, 2024. (Photo by Anne-Christine POUJOULAT / AFP)

Remco Evenepoelfollowed up his third place in the Tour de France with Olympic time trial gold on Saturday, battering his opponents on the wet, testing course in Paris.



The Belgian rider set the fastest-ever Olympic TT pace in winning the title, going 1.5km per hour faster than Bradley Wiggins did in London a dozen years earlier.

Evenepoel beat ItalianFilippo Gannaby 14.92 seconds, with his compatriotWout Van Aertthird at 25.63 seconds.

British riderJosh Tarlinghad a fine ride, netting fourth despite a bike change after a puncture. He was 27.79 seconds behind and will rue his misfortune.

American riderBrandon McNultywas fifth, with Stefan Bissegger (Switzerland) sixth.

Evenepoel is already the world champion in the discipline, and now adds Olympic success to his list of achievements.

“I cannot describe the feeling I had on the podium,” he said. “It’s a magical moment in my life.

“We watched the women’s race before we got into our warm-up and we saw the crashes so we decided not to take any risks in the corners and to push a bit more on the straights, and it was a good strategy.

“With a third spot in the Tour de France and a victory here, tiredness doesn’t matter. The motivation and the positive feelings overtake everything.”

Ganna was just over ten seconds back. Despite going so close to a gold medal, he was satisfied with his ride.

“It is better than the last Games. So I am really happy,” he said. “As everyone knows, I am not super strong with the rain, with not good weather. But in the end I tried to follow my dream, I tried to follow the pacing and everything. So I am really happy for today.

“Obviously just a few seconds is a little bit of pain, but in the end we try tonight to celebrate the news of second place.”

As for Van Aert, he too was happy.

“I really dreamed of taking a medal in the time trial,” he said, according to Sporza. “It’s ‘only’ bronze, but there are few who can say that. I am very proud of that.”

He used two disk wheels in the test and said afterward that these made a difference, helping him to beat Tarling. The latter’s puncture was of course also a factor in the two second gap between them.

“I was confident it was the right choice. I was always comfortable,” he explained.

“According to the tests, it benefits up to 17 watts. That is considerable, because all the little things like the helmet and the clothes are always one watt extra.”



The men’s Olympic time trial was run off in the same testing conditions as the women but, perhaps informed by the crashes the latter had experienced before them, were less plagued by falls.

The riders still had to exercise caution on the course, avoiding the white lines where possible and taking the safest possible lines through the many corners.

Despite that Magnus Sheffied (USA) was unlucky to be involved in a crash. British favorite Josh Tarling was delayed for another reason, needing a bike change after a puncture.

This saw him only fourth at the first checkpoint, with several of the big names still to go through that point, leaving him on the back foot and trying to make up time as the TT continued.

Wout Van Aert (Belgium) was tearing around the course, setting provisional best times at the first and second time checks. However first Ganna and then Evenepoel went quicker at the first intermediate, threatening his gold medal prospects.

Stefan Bissegger (Switzerland) raced into the finish in a time of 37:38.57 for provisional best. Van Aert powered home very soon afterwards to go just over a minute faster, with his dual disk wheels appearing to pay off.

However Ganna was on a flyer and improved that time by ten seconds, bumping the Belgian down a slot. The Italian was in the virtual hot seat for the briefest of moments, though, with Evenepoel going a further 15 seconds faster, securing gold.

The result follow up on his world championship title last year and his Tour de France TT stage win earlier this month, showing he has emerged from the Tour in fine shape.
title: Remco Evenepoel Wins Olympic TT Ahead of Ganna and Van Aert
author: Shane Stokes
description: Belgian rider Evenepoel takes Olympic gold six days after finishing in podium in first Tour de France, adding to world championship title.
